note_data.title: Energy, work and power
keyPoints: **Work done** by a constant force equals force × displacement in the direction of force (W = Fs cos θ)
**Kinetic energy** of a particle is ½mv² where m is mass and v is speed
**Gravitational potential energy** is mgh above a chosen reference level
**Work-energy theorem**: work done by all forces equals change in kinetic energy
**Conservation of mechanical energy**: KE + PE = constant when only conservative forces act
**Power** is the rate of doing work, measured in watts (P = W/t)
**Instantaneous power** equals force × velocity in direction of force (P = Fv cos θ)
Energy is always **conserved** but may be converted between different forms
Non-conservative forces like **friction** convert mechanical energy to heat energy

definitions.definition: Work done by a force is the product of the force and the displacement in the direction of the force
The energy possessed by a body due to its motion, equal to ½mv²
The energy possessed by a body due to its position in a force field
The rate of doing work or the rate of energy transfer
A force for which the work done is independent of the path taken and depends only on initial and final positions

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the principle or formula in one clear sentence without derivation
Show all working steps clearly, include units in final answer
Start from given information and work step by step to reach the required result
State the physics principle and give reasoning using 'because' or 'since'

modelAnswer.question: A block of mass 2 kg slides down a smooth inclined plane of height 5 m from rest. Calculate (a) the speed at the bottom, (b) the kinetic energy at the bottom.
modelAnswer.answer:
(a) Using conservation of energy: mgh = ½mv²
gh = ½v²
10 × 5 = ½v²
v² = 100
v = 10 m/s

(b) KE = ½mv² = ½ × 2 × 100 = 100 J
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Apply conservation of energy principle

commonMistakes: Never confuse work and energy - work is energy transferred, energy is capacity to do work
Students often forget that work = F × s × cos θ, not just F × s when force is at an angle
Don't use v = u + at when energy methods are more appropriate for the question
Never say 'energy is lost' - always say 'energy is converted to heat/sound' etc.
Students mix up instantaneous power P = Fv with average power P = W/t
examinerTips: Energy questions appear in every Paper 4, often worth 8-12 marks
Always state which energy principle you are using (conservation, work-energy theorem)
Draw energy bar charts or diagrams when asked to explain energy changes
Check units carefully - work and energy in joules (J), power in watts (W)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Energy diagram showing initial and final positions with KE and PE values labeled, plus forces acting on the system. Include reference level for potential energy.
